title: TBM 418: Campfires, Trails, and Quests
questions: How can ai help teams do more effective product work without increasing blind spots?
What is lost when someone uses ai in isolation instead of collaborating with others?
How do shared context and co-prompting change the quality of team decisions?
How can teams design an ai-enabled workflow that keeps traces and conversations in sync?
summary: This post argues that ai’s value for product development depends on whether it supports multiplayer collaboration or accelerates single-player isolation. It uses the 4Es of cognition and the idea of stigmergy to explain how shared context, co-prompting, and trace-based coordination compound into higher-leverage team understanding. It’s aimed at product teams and practitioners designing ai-enabled workflows that keep “trails” and “campfires” in a productive loop.

title: TBM 417: Before You Fire All Your Glue People Because of AI
questions: When is using AI to reduce effort actually the right intervention versus solving the wrong problem?
What invisible work do “glue people” perform that isn’t captured by the artifacts they produce?
How can we tell whether the bottleneck is friction, capability, motivation, or social opportunity?
If AI makes outputs cheap to generate, what downstream workflow and incentives determine whether anyone can use them?
search_terms: social opportunity and legitimacy in organizations
summary: This post argues that using AI to help teams do the work they “should” do is often correct, but it fails when the real barrier isn’t time or friction. It’s aimed at leaders and team members considering layoffs or automation of informal “glue people,” warning that AI can reproduce artifacts while removing judgment, legitimacy-building, and social/organizational mechanisms. The post provides a COM-B lens and a “diagnose before you automate” approach to predict when constraint shifts will undermine outcomes.

title: TBM 414: Legibility and Legitimacy
questions: When AI makes organizations more legible, who gains control and who loses it?
What makes AI-enabled control legitimate rather than merely visible and efficient?
Is organizational hierarchy primarily an information-routing problem, or a power/coordination structure?
Does AI-driven “world modeling” empower people at the edge, or turn them into operators within a tightly controlled system?
summary: This post argues that AI-driven transparency and “world model” thinking can increase organizational legibility in ways that enable control, but it often fails to address whether that control is legitimate. It is aimed at people using AI to improve team work and organizational design, urging them to question underlying values and power shifts rather than assume feasibility equals empowerment.

title: TBM 412: Institutionalized Overload (Now With AI)
questions: How does organizational overload become normalized and defended over time?
Why does new technology like AI often amplify existing work dynamics instead of changing them?
What mechanisms turn external pressure into internalized self-exploitation?
How can teams use AI without expanding context and workload further?
search_terms: work expands to fill available space
AI governance power and decision flow
summary: This post argues that organizational overload becomes institutionalized—normalized, defended, and sustained by the people inside it—and that AI tools often amplify this pattern rather than enabling deeper effectiveness. It is aimed at leaders and teams trying to use AI for more productive work, warning that “coping” with overload can become the new definition of success. The post frames resisting the expansion of context and inputs as a core part of responsible AI adoption.

title: TBM 411: Messy Docs As Helpful Pattern
questions: Where do messy working artifacts already function as a team’s scratchpad in my environment?
What thinking and coordination does the messy documentation capture that tickets and structured tools cannot?
Why do experienced teams tolerate ambiguity and keep the reflection loop alive long enough to make it work?
How can leaders achieve organizational legibility without forcing teams to abandon their emergent way of working?
search_terms: minimally viable consistency for product work
summary: This post explores why many high-performing product teams rely on messy, freeform documents that get repeatedly copied forward and reflected on, treating them as a shared scratchpad for sensemaking. It argues that the practice works through externalizing working memory and closing the loop frequently enough to become a ritual, while also raising the organizational challenge of achieving legibility without forcing teams into overly clean, ticket-only artifacts. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams designing documentation, coordination routines, and interfaces—especially in the context of how AI may reshape these workflows.

title: TBM 406: Seeing Everything, Understanding Nothing (The Context Trap)
questions: Why does adding more AI-generated context sometimes fail to produce clarity or alignment?
Is context something we can transmit ahead of time, or is it something teams create through interaction?
How should leaders communicate differently if understanding emerges from enactment rather than decoding?
When is it appropriate to document and retrieve context versus when must context be created in-the-moment?
summary: This post argues that the “context trap” in AI and leadership comes from treating context as a transferable input that can be merged to produce understanding. It reframes context as something teams enact through interaction, drawing on 4E cognition and distributed cognition to explain why broadcast-style communication and pre-read assumptions often fail. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to use AI to improve collaboration and decision-making without falling into context overload.

title: TBM 405: Hope, Context, and Control
questions: When should leaders rely on systems of record, and when do they create a context trap?
How can teams avoid rollup fixation while still meeting legitimate needs for legibility and risk reduction?
Can ai expand mētis and collective sensemaking without turning into hyperaggregation and control?
What signals distinguish ai-enabled collaboration from ai-enabled top-down management?
summary: This post argues that “systems of record” and rollup reporting create legibility at the cost of local context, which can distort decision-making in complex product development. It frames the current tech moment—pressure for efficiency, innovation, and control—as an intensified tension between institutional legibility and mētis-based sensemaking, then explores how ai can either catalyze collaboration or reinforce top-down control. It’s aimed at leaders and teams trying to use ai to improve real work without losing the nuance that makes outcomes possible.

title: TBM 392: When (And How) Tools Matter
questions: When do tools actually improve adoption of a behavior in an organization?
How do you choose the right tool intervention based on what’s blocking the behavior?
What should you do when a behavior is aspirational, inconsistent, friction-filled, or actively suppressed?
How should AI be used differently across capability, opportunity, and motivation gaps?
search_terms: political risk social norms behavior change
summary: This post explains how to decide whether tools will help (and how) by treating organizational behaviors as being in different states on a realization spectrum. It uses the COM-B model to map specific blockers—capability, opportunity, and motivation—to the kinds of tool interventions that work. It’s aimed at practitioners designing change, workflows, or AI support who need a state-dependent way to choose interventions rather than assuming tools are universally helpful.

title: TBM 385: "We Have To Simplify!"
questions: When someone says 'we need to simplify,' what do they actually mean in terms of priorities, processes, or structure?
How do you reduce bureaucracy and reporting without losing necessary governance?
What practical steps reduce silos and dependencies so teams can work with less coordination overhead?
How should AI be used in simplification efforts—what should it complement versus replace?
search_terms: AI for productivity vs process change
adapt practices to your environment not copy
summary: The post explores how the phrase 'we have to simplify' can mask multiple, conflicting interpretations—ranging from reducing priorities to cutting bureaucracy, silos, paperwork, and cognitive load. It is aimed at leaders and operators trying to turn vague calls for simplification into concrete operating-model changes, while cautioning against relying on AI or copying other companies without clarifying the real constraint.

title: TBM 329: Deep vs. Broad Context Traps
questions: When my pattern-sensing radar goes off, am I overgeneralizing from prior experience?
Where did my foundational beliefs about “how things work” come from, and do they still hold in this context?
Which “first principles” do I assume apply everywhere, and what context factors must pair with them to work?
Am I arriving at the same diagnosis because it helps, or because it’s the pattern I’m most comfortable recognizing?
summary: This post explores the risks of relying on either deep-but-narrow experience or broad-but-shallow experience when diagnosing and navigating new situations. It argues that both “pattern recognition” and “universal first principles” can fail because environments shape outcomes in context-specific ways. It’s aimed at professionals who want better judgment across organizations, roles, and industries.
